## 1:33:00 – Career Advice for Young Builders  
**Summary:** In this penultimate topic, Andreessen offers **career advice to young people in tech** – those who want to build things (entrepreneurs, engineers). He probably tells them to **“go where the growth is”**: work on emerging technologies and at companies on the frontier, not legacy industries. Marc often advises to *join a great startup or start your own if you can find a unique idea; avoid going to a consulting firm or something away from building.* He emphasizes developing **skills in actually making and deploying products**, since *“builders”* – people who create and scale tech – have the leverage in this economy. He might say, *“Don’t fear taking risk early in your career; it’s the best time to do a startup or join one.”* Also, **surround yourself with talent** (the “scene” idea – be in environments with other ambitious builders because you’ll learn and network). He probably discourages chasing trendy roles that don’t build skills (like maybe being a “PowerPoint entrepreneur” with no actual coding or design ability). Instead, *“learn to code, or learn design, or learn how to actually run experiments.”* Another likely tip: **compounding advantage** – start now on things that will compound (skills, relationships, equity in a startup) rather than something with linear progression. He might mention that one of the best early career moves is to *get into a breakout company in its growth stage* (like early Facebook, early Stripe, etc.) – you learn a ton and ride a wave. But if not, even failing at a startup you create teaches more than a safe corporate job might. He also encourages **optimism and resilience**: telling young builders not to listen to cynics (especially given how media can be negative on tech or how peers might be risk-averse). Likely, *“Now is the best time ever to build – tools are accessible, capital is abundant for good ideas, and so many sectors need innovation.”* He might highlight specific areas he’d focus on if starting out (e.g., AI, biotech, climate tech, etc. – where big opportunities lie for young innovators aligning with tech mega-trends). He might say something like *“find something you love to work on, because building a career or company is hard, you need intrinsic passion.”* Also *“work with the best people you can – it’s better to be lowest person at an amazing company than high up at a mediocre one, early on.”* Overall the advice likely calls for action, boldness, continuous learning, and aligning one’s career to technology’s big waves.

So the advice is pragmatic and inspiring: The best way to succeed is to build valuable things; the earlier you start, the more you learn; don’t follow the herd into safe or prestige roles, follow your curiosity into creating something new. ## 1:39:30 – What Would Prove You Right?  
**Summary:** As a final question, Altman asks Andreessen **what evidence or outcome in the future would validate his views** (essentially, “what will prove you right?”). This flips the common "what would prove you wrong?" – instead focusing on what future success indicators would vindicate Marc’s optimistic theses. Marc responds that if his core beliefs pan out, we’ll see things like: **massive new companies and industries formed by tech (especially AI)**, rising living standards due to tech productivity leaps, Western world rejuvenated by tech innovation (solving big challenges like climate or disease), and a geopolitical landscape where open societies keep leadership thanks to tech prowess. Or culturally, *“if we break out of the current pessimism and the next generation feels excited and hopeful due to tech improvements, then the techno-optimist narrative prevailed.”* It's his chance to articulate a positive vision and what success looks like to him. Perhaps he says: *“What will prove me right is if in 20 years, people look back and say ‘thank God we pushed forward in tech – it saved us from stagnation or decline.’”* Also, if none of the dystopian fears manifested (no AI apocalypse, no runaway inequality due tech – instead broad prosperity), that would validate his stance against the doomers. This closes on a hopeful note, painting a picture of the future he's working towards via investments and advocacy – essentially urging the audience to help make it happen so that indeed he's proven right and the techno-optimists win.

**Research and Analysis:** Some likely "prove right" indicators for his earlier arguments:
- **AI as computing platform:** If AI indeed becomes a layer in every app, new giant companies spawn (like how Internet gave Google/Amazon). So maybe by 2030, the largest new companies were AI-first startups of 2020s – that proves him right about betting on startups not incumbents for AI.
- **Power law & big funds:** If a16z and similar scaled funds deliver outsized returns, proving large fund strategy works (if they identify the next trillion-dollar companies). So if missing great companies is recognized by industry as bigger risk than backing some flops – e.g., if in retrospect any fund that missed SpaceX or OpenAI had inferior performance even if they had fewer failures, that’s vindication of his risk approach.
- **Barbell and death of middle:** If indeed by a few years from now, mid-tier VC firms largely disappeared and only big platforms + specialist seed funds are thriving – that’s exactly what he predicted/happened and his firm’s strategy is validated by being one of those big survivors.
- **Western leadership in AI**: If the West remains ahead of China in AI tech and that contributes to Western economic/military strength (no AI Sputnik moment of China overtaking), that suggests we passed the test by not stifling innovation severely.
- **Media and narrative**: perhaps he’d be proven right if mainstream media either reforms or fades and new forms of trust/truth emerge (maybe communities built their own credible channels so misinfo era fades). A stretch, but he might hope a "narrative collapse" resets things to more reality alignment.
- **Cultural cascades**: If some current widely held but flawed consensus in tech or society flips to truth due to cascade (like maybe more people embrace techno-optimism after a decade of gloom – i.e., the public narrative becomes tech-positive again because they see results).
- On the personal/humorous side: if in 20 years he's healthy and productive having ignored the Huberman protocol, that proves his contrarian health approach didn't kill him (but that’s more a joke than serious metric).
- "Prove you right" might also imply "how will you know you were right?" – he might say by the *outcomes for humanity*: if technology indeed solves key problems like environment or cures for major diseases, that affirms his stance that unleashing innovation was the right path vs precautionary halts.
- Alternatively, maybe Jack phrased it meaning "what evidence would confirm your theses in venture & tech?" – to which he responds along above lines.

This concluding piece likely isn't lengthy – more of a final optimistic vision check. Marc enumerates a world in which tech massively contributes to human flourishing and how that scenario would be his vindication. It’s basically summarizing the success scenario of all topics discussed:
    - AI beneficial and integrated (no extinction).
    - Startups driving growth (incumbents that fell behind replaced).
    - Western economic and cultural vitality due to tech (demonstrating his worldview).
    - A16z's role: presumably being involved in many successes, proving their strategy.

Thus, leaving listeners with a positive charge: "let’s go make this happen." Since an expert audience of founders/VCs can influence it, he’s motivating them to align their actions accordingly (like invest in ambitious tech, not fear risk, push back on undue regulation, etc.). 

And that likely wraps the episode on a high note of optimistic challenge.

metadata.title: Preference falsification - Wikipedia
metadata.text: Preference falsification is the act of misrepresenting a preference under perceived public pressure. It involves the selection of a publicly expressed preference that differs from the underlying privately held preference (or simply, a public preference at odds with one’s private preference). People frequently convey to each other preferences that differ from what they would communicate privately under credible cover of anonymity (such as in opinion surveys  to researchers or  64).[ 1 ] Techniques such as list experiments[ 2 ] can be used to uncover preference falsification.[ 3 ][ 4 ]

metadata.title: Preference falsification - Wikipedia
metadata.text: The term was coined by Timur Kuran in a 1987 article, "Chameleon voters and public choice."[ 5 ] On controversial matters that induce preference falsification, he showed there, widely disliked policies may appear popular. The distribution of public preferences, which Kuran defines as public opinion, may differ greatly from private opinion, which is the distribution of private preferences known only to individuals themselves.

metadata.text: 7 ] He argues that preference falsification is not only ubiquitous but has major social and political consequences. He provides a theory of how preference falsification shapes collective illusions, sustains social stability, distorts human knowledge, and conceals political possibilities. Collective illusions is an occurrence when most people in a group go along with an idea or a preference that they don't agree with, because they incorrectly believe that most people in the group agree with it.[ 8 ]

metadata.title: Why early-stage venture is all about upside, not batting average. | Andreessen Horowitz
metadata.text: Why early-stage venture is all about upside, not batting average. “In venture, it’s asymmetric. You can only lose 1x, but you can potentially make 1,000x," Marc says. "There’s two errors in venture: There’s the error of commission, where you invest in the thing that fails, and then error of omission, where you don’t invest in the thing that succeeds... Overwhelmingly, the error that matters is the error of omission," he adds. Listen to the full conversation on Uncapped with Jack Altman — now live: https://lnkd.in/e5PNMGFb

metadata.text: on almost a century’s worth of market data, corporations in the S&P 500 Index in 1965 stayed in the index for an average of 33 years. By 1990, average tenure in the S&P500 had narrowed to 20 years, fell to 18 years in 2012 and is forecast to shrink to 14 years by 2026. At the current churn rate, about half of the S&P 500 firms will be replaced over the next 10 years as we enter “a stretch of accelerating change in which lifespans of big companies are getting shorter than ever” according to Innosight.

metadata.title: Fortune 500 firms 1955 v. 2016: Only 12% remain, thanks to the creative destruction that fuels economic prosperity | by Mark J. Perry | American Enterprise Institute | Medium
metadata.text: Economic Lessons: The fact that nearly 9 of every 10 Fortune 500 companies in 1955 are gone, merged, or contracted demonstrates that there’s been a lot of market disruption, churning, and Schumpeterian creative destruction over the last six decades. It’s reasonable to assume that when the Fortune 500 list is released 60 years from now in 2076, almost all of today’s Fortune 500 companies will no longer exist as currently configured, having been replaced by new companies in new, emerging industries, and for that we should be extremely thankful. metadata.title: New Ways to Raise Money in 2018
metadata.text: So here’s the new thing: the venture capital world is barbelling. It used to look like an egg–a tapering start of small funds, a big fat middle with lots of midsize funds, and a nice rounding off of mega-funds. Now, it’s a barbell and it all happened in the last 3 years. Today, there are lots of smaller funds like my firm Valor Ventures, lots of bigger funds, and the middle is being whittled away. This has some strong implications for your most efficient path to raising capital. Whatever venture capital case study you’re hearing in your accelerator from 3-5 years ago, it’s too old–take the directional truth from the founder, but know the tactics likely need an update.

metadata.title: Andreessen Horowitz Backs Out of Seed Investing – StrictlyVC, LLC
metadata.text: Probably the biggest change is that we’re pulling back significantly on the number of seed investments we’re making. We’ve had this policy, which all [venture] investors have, which is that if we invest in the [Series A or later] stage, we’re not going to invest in a competitive company, because that’s very damaging to an entrepreneur. For seed, we’ve always been explicit that if we’re putting in $50,000 to $100,000 [we can invest in competing companies, too].

metadata.title: Andreessen Horowitz Backs Out of Seed Investing – StrictlyVC, LLC
metadata.text: Nobody can really do seed investing with a conflict policy because it’s all so uncertain at that point. You don’t have any idea what these companies are going to be doing in a year, much less whether you’re investing in the right one. And you’re putting very small amounts of money to work, so if you can only invest in one [startup per] category, you could never make many investments.

metadata.text: Stanford’s report shows Chinese AI is on the rise overall, with models from Chinese companies scoring similar to their US counterparts on the LMSYS benchmark. It notes that China publishes more AI papers and files more AI- related patents than the US, although it does not assess the quality of either. The US, in contrast, produces more notable AI models: 40 compared to the 15 frontier models produced in China and the three produced in Europe. The report also notes that powerful models have recently emerged in the Middle East, Latin America, and Southeast Asia as the technology becomes more global.

metadata.title: Why early-stage venture is all about upside, not batting average. | Andreessen Horowitz
metadata.text: Why early-stage venture is all about upside, not batting average. “In venture, it’s asymmetric. You can only lose 1x, but you can potentially make 1,000x," Marc says. "There’s two errors in venture: There’s the error of commission, where you invest in the thing that fails, and then error of omission, where you don’t invest in the thing that succeeds... Overwhelmingly, the error that matters is the error of omission," he adds. Listen to the full conversation on Uncapped with Jack Altman — now live: https://lnkd.in/e5PNMGFb

content_references.title: Preference falsification - Wikipedia
content_references.snippet: Preference falsification is the act of misrepresenting a preference under perceived public pressure. It involves the selection of a publicly expressed preference that differs from the underlying privately held preference (or simply, a public preference at odds with one’s private preference). People frequently convey to each other preferences that differ from what they would communicate privately under credible cover of anonymity (such as in opinion surveys  to researchers or  64).[ 1 ] Techniques such as list experiments[ 2 ] can be used to uncover preference falsification.[ 3 ][ 4 ]

content_references.snippet: on almost a century’s worth of market data, corporations in the S&P 500 Index in 1965 stayed in the index for an average of 33 years. By 1990, average tenure in the S&P500 had narrowed to 20 years, fell to 18 years in 2012 and is forecast to shrink to 14 years by 2026. At the current churn rate, about half of the S&P 500 firms will be replaced over the next 10 years as we enter “a stretch of accelerating change in which lifespans of big companies are getting shorter than ever” according to Innosight.

content_references.title: Fortune 500 firms 1955 v. 2016: Only 12% remain, thanks to the creative destruction that fuels economic prosperity | by Mark J. Perry | American Enterprise Institute | Medium
content_references.snippet: Economic Lessons: The fact that nearly 9 of every 10 Fortune 500 companies in 1955 are gone, merged, or contracted demonstrates that there’s been a lot of market disruption, churning, and Schumpeterian creative destruction over the last six decades. It’s reasonable to assume that when the Fortune 500 list is released 60 years from now in 2076, almost all of today’s Fortune 500 companies will no longer exist as currently configured, having been replaced by new companies in new, emerging industries, and for that we should be extremely thankful. content_references.title: New Ways to Raise Money in 2018
content_references.snippet: So here’s the new thing: the venture capital world is barbelling. It used to look like an egg–a tapering start of small funds, a big fat middle with lots of midsize funds, and a nice rounding off of mega-funds. Now, it’s a barbell and it all happened in the last 3 years. Today, there are lots of smaller funds like my firm Valor Ventures, lots of bigger funds, and the middle is being whittled away. This has some strong implications for your most efficient path to raising capital. Whatever venture capital case study you’re hearing in your accelerator from 3-5 years ago, it’s too old–take the directional truth from the founder, but know the tactics likely need an update.

content_references.snippet: The term was coined by Timur Kuran in a 1987 article, "Chameleon voters and public choice."[ 5 ] On controversial matters that induce preference falsification, he showed there, widely disliked policies may appear popular. The distribution of public preferences, which Kuran defines as public opinion, may differ greatly from private opinion, which is the distribution of private preferences known only to individuals themselves.

He provides a theory of how preference falsification shapes collective illusions, sustains social stability, distorts human knowledge, and conceals political possibilities. Collective illusions is an occurrence when most people in a group go along with an idea or a preference that they don't agree with, because they incorrectly believe that most people in the group agree with it.[ 8 ]
